concepts.title: Varna System
concepts.description: The Varna system is a social hierarchy in Hinduism that divides society into four main groups: Brahmans (priests), Kshatriyas (warriors), Vaishyas (merchants), and Shudras (labourers).

concepts.title: Caste System vs Caste
concepts.description: The caste system refers to the rigid social hierarchy in ancient India, while caste refers to a specific group within this hierarchy.

concepts.title: Emergence of New Caste Groups
concepts.description: New caste groups emerged in ancient India due to the rise of new occupations, such as artisans, craftsmen, and traders.

concepts.title: Varna System Mnemonic
concepts.description: Brahmans - B (Brainy and wise), Kshatriyas - K (Kingly and brave), Vaishyas - V (Vast and wealthy), Shudras - S (Skilled and hardworking)

concepts.title: Confusion between Varna and Caste
concepts.description: Students often confuse the Varna system with the caste system. However, the Varna system is a social hierarchy, while the caste system refers to specific groups within this hierarchy.

concepts.title: Jati System
concepts.description: The Jati system is a localised and flexible social hierarchy in ancient India, where people were grouped based on their occupations and geographical locations.

concepts.title: Impact of Foreign Rule on Indian Society
concepts.description: The foreign rule in India, such as the British, had a significant impact on Indian society, leading to the decline of the traditional caste system and the emergence of new social classes.

concepts.title: Key Features of the Varna System
concepts.description: Key features of the Varna system include: division of society into four main groups, hereditary occupation, and a rigid social hierarchy.
